text.title: Roadmap Structure and Format
text.summary: Time-based vs theme-based roadmap tradeoffs, Now/Next/Later structure with confidence levels, and antipatterns that undermine roadmap usefulness.

rule.statement: Use theme-based roadmaps for solo projects. Use time-based roadmaps for teams with committed delivery windows and external stakeholders. Never use both simultaneously in the same document.
rule.rationale: Theme-based roadmaps are more stable — themes do not shift when quarters slip. Time-based roadmaps create false commitment pressure that causes rushed decisions when dates approach.

section.title: Now/Next/Later Structure

rule.statement: Now items must have a target milestone and status (On Track / At Risk / Delayed). Next items must have a confidence level (High/Medium/Low). Later items carry only thematic groupings — no dates, no status.
rule.rationale: Precision decreases with planning horizon. Forcing concrete dates on Later items creates false precision for work that will change as earlier work lands and requirements evolve.

rule.statement: Re-run grooming every 4-6 weeks, not once per quarter. Later items drift; roadmaps not refreshed regularly become obstacles rather than guides.
rule.rationale: Quarterly roadmap reviews are too infrequent for fast-moving projects. Items in Later bucket will have changed priority; failing to update the roadmap means the team is working against stale priorities.

section.reference: Roadmap skeleton with Now/Next/Later sections, Not Planned list, Dependencies, Change Log.

rule.statement: Document items explicitly excluded from the roadmap with a reason. A "Won't do" list prevents repeated re-prioritization of the same rejected idea.
rule.rationale: Without an explicit exclusion list, rejected ideas re-enter grooming sessions on each cycle. Documenting why they were rejected short-circuits the debate.

antipattern.description: Roadmap treated as a commitment: "We promised Q2 for this feature."
antipattern.reason: Roadmap is a plan, not a promise. It must be updated as learning occurs. Treating it as a commitment prevents honest updates when circumstances change.

antipattern.description: Too many P0/Must items (more than 3 simultaneous "most important" features).
antipattern.reason: If everything is most important, nothing is. Forces hard prioritization decisions before the roadmap is published.

antipattern.description: Grooming session run as a status update meeting.
antipattern.reason: Grooming focuses on prioritization, not updates. Status belongs in stand-up or async reports. Combining them dilutes both.
